Handle: Polynomial from a sector into a strip
Estimated MOHS: 15M
IMO slot guess: P1/4
Topic tags: Alg/CA – polynomials over C; asymptotic leading term; geometric image condition
Core ideas:
Rotate B into a bounded-real-part strip
Look along rays in the sector and compare with the leading term
A nonconstant polynomial cannot stay inside a fixed strip on an unbounded sector
Rationale (1–2 lines): Once you translate the condition into “bounded real part on a whole sector,” the leading-term argument is standard and decisive. The search space is tiny after the right rephrasing.
Common pitfalls: Missing the strip-rotation trick; overcomplicating with Liouville/entire-function language when a raywise leading-term check already kills nonconstant cases.
Confidence: High
Notes (optional): This is short and clean, but not “magic.” The wedge angle is mostly a red herring.
Handle: Cyclic quadrilateral with P, Q, K, T
Estimated MOHS: 45M
IMO slot guess: P3/6
Topic tags: Geo – cyclic quadrilateral; pole/polar; power of a point; projective/metric mix
Core ideas:
Introduce R = AD ∩ BC and exploit complete-quadrilateral structure
Use cyclicity plus power relations on the circumcircle and the auxiliary circle through P, A, D, K
Bring in pole/polar geometry with respect to the unit circle to control Q and OP
Convert the metric condition on K into a rigid configuration
Rationale (1–2 lines): This looks synthetic, but the real burden is finding the correct hidden infrastructure. The key auxiliary point(s) and the polar viewpoint are not standard “first try” moves, so the intuition cost is very high.
Common pitfalls: Chasing angles locally around K and Q without finding the global projective structure; trying brute-force coordinates too early and drowning in algebra.
Confidence: Medium
Notes (optional): If one spots the right pole/polar package very early, it can feel like 40M; without that, it is a genuine 45M-type geometry problem.
Handle: Red-blue cards with repeated averaging
Estimated MOHS: 40M
IMO slot guess: P3/6
Topic tags: Comb/Alg – dynamical process; extremal construction; invariant/potential; averaging
Core ideas:
Find the right monotone potential; naive sum/variance alone is too weak
Understand that locally greedy averaging is not optimal
Build a sharp or near-sharp recursive strategy for the lower bound
Match it with a non-obvious upper bound from the same potential framework
Rationale (1–2 lines): The statement is elementary, but the winning invariant is not. This is exactly the kind of short process problem whose true hardness comes from hidden structure rather than computation.
Common pitfalls: Assuming the best strategy is “always lift the smallest red by the largest blue”; using only total sum or variance and getting a bound far from sharp.
Confidence: Medium
Notes (optional): This is the type of problem whose MOHS jumps upward because the successful potential is hard to invent from scratch.
Handle: 70-card stack, 30 colors, top-50/bottom-20
Estimated MOHS: 35M
IMO slot guess: P2/5
Topic tags: Comb – invariant/monovariant; extremal process; weighted potential; exact maximum
Core ideas:
Encode the top-50 by a color-multiplicity/position profile
Prove finiteness with a weighted lexicographic potential, not just a raw color-count argument
Construct a worst-case initial arrangement that nearly saturates the potential
Show the exact maximum by matching upper and lower bounds
Rationale (1–2 lines): The process is combinatorially rich, but the space of legal moves is structured enough that a strong monovariant should exist. The hard part is inventing the right one and then making it sharp.
Common pitfalls: Tracking only the number of distinct colors in the top 50; ignoring positional information, which is where the true difficulty sits.
Confidence: Medium-Low
Notes (optional): Could move up to 40M if the exact extremal construction is especially delicate.
Handle: Determine all λ in the symmetric inequality
Estimated MOHS: 35M
IMO slot guess: P2/5
Topic tags: Alg – symmetric inequalities; elementary symmetric sums; asymptotic extremals; smoothing
Core ideas:
Rewrite everything in terms of e2, e3 and the variance-type term
Identify that the large-n extremal behavior is driven by near-uniform configurations
Prove sufficiency with a stability-style inequality, not just the limiting heuristic
Handle the “there exists N” quantifier carefully
Rationale (1–2 lines): The asymptotic quantifier makes this subtler than a routine symmetric inequality. The hard part is not expansion; it is locating the true extremal regime and proving the buffer is enough for all large n.
Common pitfalls: Treating it as a plain fixed-n uvw problem; proving only the limiting constant and forgetting the eventual-in-n requirement.
Confidence: Medium
Notes (optional): If one immediately normalizes into symmetric-sum language and sees the near-uniform extremal, it drops closer to 30M.
Handle: Recurrence-permutation triples in Z_n
Estimated MOHS: 40M
IMO slot guess: P3/6
Topic tags: NT/Alg/Comb – linear recurrences mod n; CRT; characteristic polynomial; permutation structure
Core ideas:
Guess/recognize the affine-permutation family coming from a second-difference relation
Show non-affine characteristic-root behavior is impossible under the condition gcd(p-1, n)=1
Reduce modulo prime powers / use CRT to globalize the classification
Count only scalar multiples that survive the gcd condition
Rationale (1–2 lines): The answer is likely rigid, but proving rigidity is the whole problem. The bridge from “permutation of residues” to “characteristic roots must collapse” is a deep structural leap.
Common pitfalls: Brute-forcing sequence patterns instead of classifying the recurrence; not exploiting the special condition on prime divisors of n.
Confidence: Medium
Notes (optional): If one has seen companion-matrix or root-of-unity killing arguments in modular recurrences, this can feel closer to 35M; otherwise it is very hard to discover.
